summary: I was born and raised in Bhutan, spending the first 13 to 14 years of my life there. I grew up in a very rural countryside where my parents were farmers, and I walked two hours to school every day. During my childhood, Bhutan was undergoing political turmoil, especially with the government's ethnic cleansing policies against Nepali-speaking people. Despite the challenges, my early years in Bhutan were filled with family support and a close-knit community.

Though I was originally from Bhutan, I now live in Columbus, Ohio. This city represents a new chapter in my life after fleeing Bhutan due to political persecution. Columbus has been my home where I have settled after the difficult journey and displacement from my birthplace.

After being forced to leave Bhutan in 1992 due to ethnic cleansing, my family and I fled to Nepal seeking refuge. We traveled through India and finally reached Nepal, where there were already refugee camps sponsored by UNHCR. Although we spoke the same language, life in Nepal was uncertain and challenging as we had lost everything and had to start over as refugees.

During my childhood in Bhutan, the King of Bhutan implemented policies aimed at ethnic cleansing of Nepali-speaking people. The King wanted these people to either leave the country or fully assimilate by adopting Bhutanese dress, culture, and Buddhism. This royal policy deeply affected my family and community, leading to our eventual forced displacement.

I come from a Brahmin family in Bhutan, which shaped much of my religious upbringing. My parents taught us religious rituals and we recited the Gita together every morning. Being Brahmin meant that religion was an essential part of my daily life and identity, even though I sometimes felt conflicted about the social stratification it represented.

The Gita was a central part of my religious education growing up in Bhutan. Every morning, my siblings, cousins, and I would sit with my father to recite the Gita as part of our daily rituals. This practice helped instill values about life, humanity, and service that have stayed with me throughout my life.

The ethnic cleansing in Bhutan during the early 1990s was a traumatic event that forced my family and many others to flee. The government targeted Nepali-speaking people, accusing them of being foreigners and forcing them to abandon their culture or leave. I witnessed violence, intimidation, and destruction in my village, which ultimately led to our escape to Nepal in 1992.

Nepali was the language spoken by my family and community in Bhutan, but during the ethnic cleansing, speaking Nepali became a reason for discrimination. We were told to abandon our language and culture to be accepted as Bhutanese. Despite this pressure, Nepali remained a vital part of our identity, especially as we resettled in Nepal.

Buddhism was the dominant religion in Bhutan and part of the cultural identity the government wanted us to adopt. As a Brahmin family, we followed Hindu rituals, which set us apart from the Buddhist majority. The government pressured Nepali-speaking people like us to convert to Buddhism and follow Bhutanese customs, which was part of the assimilation policy.

The UNHCR played a crucial role in supporting Bhutanese refugees like my family when we fled to Nepal in 1992. They had already established refugee camps and began sponsoring refugees from 1990 onward. Their presence provided some structure and aid during a very uncertain and difficult time for us as displaced people.

During our escape from Bhutan, we traveled through West Bengal as part of the long journey to Nepal. We were transported in a large truck along with other families, moving through Indian states including West Bengal and Assam. This leg of the journey was exhausting and filled with uncertainty as we left everything behind.

Assam was one of the Indian states we passed through on our way from Bhutan to Nepal. Traveling through Assam was part of the long and difficult journey in a crowded truck with many other refugee families. The entire trip was a mix of fear, hope, and uncertainty about what awaited us in Nepal.

India was a transit country during our escape from Bhutan to Nepal. We crossed Indian territory, passing through states like West Bengal and Assam before reaching Nepal. India’s border areas were where we registered as refugees, marking a painful transition from being Bhutanese citizens to stateless refugees.
